reasoning: The Federal Reserve's long-term dot plot and macro trajectory indicate an easing cycle through 2025 and 2026, targeting a neutral rate around 3.0%. With the EFFR at 4.12% in late 2025, it is highly likely the Fed will either cut rates further or maintain them through early 2026. A rate cut (typically 25 bps) would significantly lower the EFFR, entirely overwhelming any minor daily fluctuations. Even if the Fed holds rates steady, a strict increase in the daily EFFR purely due to market fluctuations is only a marginal possibility. A complete reversal leading to a rate hike is highly improbable in this phase of the monetary policy cycle. Therefore, the likelihood of the EFFR being strictly higher by the early 2026 close date compared to November 2025 is low.
